prerequisites.question: An object in uniform circular motion experiences a force directed:
options: toward the center of the circle
away from the center of the circle
prerequisites.answer: toward the center of the circle
prerequisites.remediation: Centripetal force always points toward the center — it's what keeps an object moving in a circle rather than flying off in a straight line.

prerequisites.question: Two masses exert a gravitational force on each other. If the distance between them doubles, the force becomes:
prerequisites.remediation: Newton's law of gravitation depends on 1/r² — doubling distance means the force divides by 2² = 4.

prerequisites.question: Kepler's second law states that a line joining a planet to the Sun sweeps out equal areas in equal times. This implies the planet moves:
options: at constant speed throughout its orbit
faster when closer to the Sun
slower when closer to the Sun
at the same speed as every other planet
prerequisites.answer: faster when closer to the Sun
prerequisites.remediation: Equal areas in equal times means the planet must cover more arc length when it's nearer the Sun — so it speeds up at perihelion.

beats.title: Elliptical orbits — what they look like
beats.goal: Build intuition that planetary orbits are ellipses, not circles, and that the Sun sits at one focus.

skeleton.text: This is an ellipse, not a circle — the Sun sits at one focus, off-center. Watch how the planet's distance from the Sun changes.
The semi-major axis a is half the longest diameter — it sets the orbit's overall size.
When eccentricity e = 0, the orbit becomes a perfect circle. Now I'll bring e back up.
Try the eccentricity slider — slide it from 0 to 0.9. How does the shape change?
Where is the Sun in an elliptical orbit?
options: At the center of the ellipse
At one focus of the ellipse
At the edge of the ellipse
skeleton.answer: At one focus of the ellipse

beats.title: Kepler's Second Law — sweeping equal areas
beats.goal: Demonstrate that a planet moves faster near perihelion and slower near aphelion; connect this to equal areas in equal times.

skeleton.text: Watch the velocity vector — it grows longer when the planet is closer to the Sun and shorter when farther away.
The planet sweeps the same area in the same time regardless of where it is — so it must move faster when the radius is shorter.
Why does a planet speed up near perihelion?
options: It must sweep equal area in equal time with a shorter radius
skeleton.answer: It must sweep equal area in equal time with a shorter radius

beats.title: Newton's Law of Universal Gravitation
beats.goal: Quantify the gravitational force between two masses and connect the inverse-square law to orbital behavior.

skeleton.title: The force that holds it all together

skeleton.text: Every pair of masses attracts with a force proportional to 1 over r squared. This is the centripetal force for every orbit.
Doubling the distance cuts the force to a quarter. Tripling it cuts to a ninth. This steep drop-off shapes every orbit.
For a circular orbit, gravity supplies exactly the centripetal force needed: G M m / r² equals m v² / r.

skeleton.title: Derive orbital speed
notes: set gravity equal to centripetal force
cancel m and multiply both sides by r
take square root of both sides

skeleton.text: Notice: orbital speed depends only on the central mass M and the radius r — the planet's own mass cancels out.
If Earth were suddenly 4 times farther from the Sun, by what factor would the gravitational force decrease?

beats.title: Kepler's Third Law — periods and distances
beats.goal: Show that T² ∝ a³ for all planets orbiting the same star, and verify with a numerical example.

skeleton.text: Two planets orbiting the same star. The outer one has a semi-major axis 2.5 times larger — how much slower is its period?

skeleton.title: Kepler's Third Law
notes: for planets around the same star
ratio form: take square root of cube

skeleton.text: The outer planet takes nearly 4 times longer to complete one orbit. T² scales with a³.
Adjust the semi-major axes. For any pair, the ratio of periods follows (a_outer / a_inner) raised to the 3/2 power.
If a planet's semi-major axis is 4 AU, what is T² / a³? (Use AU and years — it should be 1 for our solar system.) Enter the value.

beats.title: Escape velocity and orbital energy
beats.goal: Derive escape speed from energy conservation and show why it's √2 times the circular orbital speed.

skeleton.text: To escape a planet's gravity, total mechanical energy must reach zero — kinetic energy must balance gravitational potential energy.

skeleton.title: Derive escape speed
notes: set total energy to zero at infinity
move potential term to right side

skeleton.text: Compare this with circular orbital speed v = sqrt(GM/r). Escape speed is sqrt(2) ≈ 1.41 times the orbital speed at the same radius.
Every body's escape speed is √2 times its circular orbital speed at the same distance. Jupiter's deep gravity well demands enormous speed to leave.
How does escape velocity compare to circular orbital velocity at the same radius?
